Perfect! Now I have a complete understanding of the app structure. Let me provide you with a comprehensive summary.

## Complete Gossamer App Directory Exploration

I've thoroughly explored the app directory. Here's the complete picture:

### 1. Overall File Structure

```
/Users/stephanostsoucas/gossamer/app/
├── src/
│   ├── main/              # Electron main process
│   │   └── index.ts
│   ├── preload/           # Electron preload (security layer)
│   │   └── index.ts
│   └── renderer/          # React UI (Electron renderer process)
│       ├── index.html     # HTML entry point
│       └── src/
│           ├── main.tsx                    # React bootstrap
│           ├── App.tsx                     # Route definitions & layout
│           ├── Layout.tsx                  # AppShell with sidebar + header
│           ├── BreadcrumbContext.tsx       # Navigation breadcrumbs
│           ├── api.ts                      # HTTP/WS API client (100+ lines)
│           ├── primitives.tsx              # Legacy primitives (deprecated)
│           ├── assets/                     # Images
│           ├── components/                 # Reusable Mantine components
│           │   ├── CheckpointMessageItem.tsx
│           │   ├── EventItem.tsx
│           │   ├── MarkdownView.tsx
│           │   ├── SessionRow.tsx
│           │   ├── TimeAgo.tsx
│           │   ├── ToolGroupItem.tsx
│           │   ├── ToolUseItem.tsx
│           │   └── diff2html-theme.css
│           └── screens/                    # Page-level containers
│               ├── ActiveSessions.tsx      # Home: Sessions + Repos view
│               ├── SessionTree.tsx         # Parent/child session tree
│               ├── SessionDetail.tsx       # Single session with 2-column layout
│               ├── Checkpoints.tsx         # List of checkpoints
│               ├── CheckpointDetail.tsx    # Single checkpoint detail
│               ├── CheckpointTimeline.tsx  # Timeline visualization
│               ├── BranchLog.tsx           # Git branch log entries
│               ├── Repos.tsx               # Repo management
│               └── Search.tsx              # Full-text search
├── out/                   # Build output (Electron build)
│   ├── main/index.js      # Compiled main process
│   ├── preload/index.js   # Compiled preload
│   └── renderer/          # Compiled React (Vite output)
│       └── index.html + assets/
├── package.json           # Node dependencies
├── tsconfig.json          # TS project references
├── tsconfig.node.json     # Node-side TS config
├── tsconfig.web.json      # React-side TS config (ES2020, DOM)
├── electron.vite.config.ts # Vite + Electron build config
```

Clean build. Here's what was added:

- **`src/main/index.ts`** — IPC handlers for `pty:spawn`, `pty:write`, `pty:resize`, `pty:kill`. Spawns a real PTY using `node-pty` (your login shell from `$SHELL`) and streams output back to the renderer.
- **`src/preload/index.ts`** — `contextBridge` exposes `window.pty` to the renderer with full isolation.
- **`src/renderer/src/screens/Terminal.tsx`** — xterm.js terminal with the fit addon (auto-resizes to fill the panel), Mantine-matching dark theme, and a `ResizeObserver` that keeps the PTY cols/rows in sync.
- **`App.tsx`** — `/terminal` route added.
- **`Layout.tsx`** — "Terminal" nav item with `>` symbol; navigating away and back spawns a fresh shell.

Note: navigating away kills the PTY session (terminal state is lost on route change). If you want the session to persist across navigation, let me know and I can add a keep-alive approach.

content.text:
Clean. Here's how the keep-alive works:

- `<Terminal active={...} />` is **always mounted** inside `Layout.tsx`, outside the `<Outlet />`, so the React router never unmounts it.
- When you navigate away, the terminal div gets `display: none` and the outlet gets `display: flex` — and vice versa.
- The `active` prop triggers a `requestAnimationFrame(() => fitAddon.fit())` when you navigate back, so xterm re-measures after the browser has re-painted the visible panel.
- The PTY process and all terminal history survive navigation completely.
